coaching.coaching: Plan how runnable verification commands for all acceptance criteria will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: runnable verification commands for all acceptance criteria

coaching.coaching: Pin down ordering and precedence of finalize_verdict elif branches with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: ordering and precedence of finalize_verdict elif branches

coaching.coaching: Plan how retry-mechanism test coverage specification will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: retry-mechanism test coverage specification

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for behavioral contract change from silent PASS to INDETERMINATE — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: behavioral contract change from silent PASS to INDETERMINATE

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on rollback risk from schema additionalProperties constraint: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: rollback risk from schema additionalProperties constraint

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for bug-tier early-return path threading gap.
coaching.subject: bug-tier early-return path threading gap

coaching.coaching: Realign relevance-exception carve-out alignment with parent epic AC4 to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.
coaching.subject: relevance-exception carve-out alignment with parent epic AC4

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` field declaration in AC #1 and Remediation item 1 from `list[str] = []` to `field(default_factory=list)` (matching the existing `children` field pattern in the same `@dataclass`).
evidence: `det_floor.py:103`: `@dataclass` decorator on `PlanContext`
`det_floor.py:114`: `children: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)` — existing parallel list field uses `field(default_factory=list)`
AC #1: 'PlanContext gains `hierarchy_incomplete: bool = False` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []`'
Remediation item 1: 'Add both fields to `PlanContext` (`det_floor.py:104-131`) with defaults `False` / `[]`'
findings.finding: `PlanContext` is a `@dataclass` (det_floor.py:103), so `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` is a mutable default that will raise `ValueError: mutable default <class 'list'> for field hierarchy_incomplete_detail is not allowed: use default_factory` at class-definition time. The plan's AC #1 and Remediation item 1 both specify the bare `= []` form, which is invalid. The existing parallel list field `children` already uses `field(default_factory=list)` as the correct pattern.
findings.location: AC #1 / Remediation item 1 — `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` in `det_floor.py`
scenarios: Implementing the plan as written causes an immediate `ValueError` when Python imports `det_floor.py`, breaking the entire plan-review subsystem at startup.
findings.suggested_fix: In `det_floor.py`, declare: `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)` (and update AC #1 wording accordingly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` must be declared with `dataclasses.field(default_factory=list)` (or an explicit `self.hierarchy_incomplete_detail = []` in `__init__`), not as a bare class-level `= []`, to prevent shared-mutable-default corruption across PlanContext instances.
evidence: Plan AC: "`PlanContext` gains `hierarchy_incomplete: bool = False` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` (`det_floor.py`)."
Remediation item 1: "append that child's id ... to `hierarchy_incomplete_detail`" — i.e. mutation via `append`, not reassignment

If PlanContext is a dataclass, bare `list[str] = []` raises ValueError; if a plain class, all instances share the class-level list object and cross-invocation append corruption is guaranteed in any process that creates more than one PlanContext (e.g. batch review or the cached assemble_context wrapper).
No `field(default_factory=list)` or instance-level initialisation pattern is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` as a new field on `PlanContext` but does not specify it must use `dataclasses.field(default_factory=list)` (or equivalent). If `PlanContext` is a dataclass and the field is declared with a bare `[]` literal, Python raises `ValueError` at class-definition time. If `PlanContext` is a plain class and the field is set as a class-level attribute `hierarchy_incomplete_detail = []`, all instances share the same list object — `_assemble_context_uncached`'s `append` calls in the per-child failure path would mutate that shared default across invocations, silently corrupting `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` for every subsequent `PlanContext` created in the same process (e.g., under the cached `assemble_context` wrapper or in batch-review scenarios).
findings.location: Remediation item 1 / AC line: "`PlanContext` gains `hierarchy_incomplete: bool = False` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []`"
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC and Remediation item 1, change the field declaration to: `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)` (for a dataclass) or add `self.hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` in `PlanContext.__init__` (for a plain class).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each group of ACs — at minimum: (a) a `pytest` invocation targeting the new/modified test module with `-k` filters matching the total-enumeration and partial-failure scenarios; (b) a `grep` or `jq` command confirming the schema declares both new properties; (c) a command (or test) exercising INDETERMINATE propagation through all three drift_floor/xcheck call sites.
evidence: AC 10: 'add a total-enumeration-failure test asserting `hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ["enumeration"]` exactly … and a partial-failure test … asserting `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` contains that specific child's ticket id' — no file path or pytest command
ACs 1–9 each describe a code-state outcome (field exists, branch present, schema property declared) but provide no `pytest tests/…` or `grep`/`jq` proving command

AC 7: 'finalize_verdict … and drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop … each gain an elif … branch; the drift_floor branch is exercised through all three call sites' — no command to verify all three call sites are exercised
findings.finding: No proving commands (pytest invocations, script paths, or equivalent runnable checks) are given for any of the ACs. AC 10 describes test-case assertion content (e.g., `hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ['enumeration']`) but names no test file, test function, or module where those tests reside, leaving an executor unable to run a targeted command that produces pass/fail evidence for the claimed behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, all ACs; Remediation item 7 (test-case content assertions)
scenarios: An executor implements the changes, runs a broad `pytest` sweep that passes, and signs off — but the specific test assertions in AC 10 (exact `['enumeration']` value, specific child id, INDETERMINATE surviving a drop) are never confirmed because no targeted command was specified.
AC 7 claims the drift_floor branch is exercised at all three call sites; without a test command or test path, an executor cannot produce evidence that all three paths are covered.

evidence: 6 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test criterion verifying the retry fires: assert that `list_tickets` (and `show_ticket`) is called **twice** on total failure (e.g., using a mock call-count check) and that `hierarchy_incomplete` is only set after exhaustion — not on the first failure.
evidence: Remediation item 7: 'add a total-enumeration-failure test asserting hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ["enumeration"] exactly' and 'a partial-failure test (one child's show_ticket raises) asserting hierarchy_incomplete_detail contains that specific child's ticket id' — neither asserts retry count or delay.
AC #2: 'retries once (2 attempts total, 50ms fixed delay, same broad Exception predicate as today) before setting hierarchy_incomplete=True' — specifies the retry as a code deliverable.
AC #3: 'The per-child show_ticket fetch gets the same bounded-retry-then-flag treatment' — same gap.
ABSENCE: No test AC says 'assert that list_tickets is called twice on total failure' or uses a call-count mock to verify the retry fires.
findings.finding: The retry mechanism (2 attempts, 50ms fixed delay) is a described deliverable in Remediation item 1 and AC #2/#3, but the test-coverage specification (Remediation item 7 and the test ACs) contains no criterion verifying that the retry actually fires before `hierarchy_incomplete` is set — only the post-retry end state (`hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ["enumeration"]`) is asserted. An implementation that sets the flag on the first failure without retrying would pass all specified tests.
findings.location: Remediation item 7 / Acceptance Criteria (test coverage bullets a–c)
scenarios: An implementer omits the retry and just catches the exception on the first attempt, sets the flag, and all test assertions still pass because the tests only check the resulting flag value.
The 50ms delay is omitted or the retry count is 3 instead of 1 — both undetectable by the specified tests.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation item 7, add: '(d) mock list_tickets to raise on every call and assert it is called exactly 2 times before hierarchy_incomplete is set; (e) mock list_tickets to raise on the first call only and assert hierarchy_incomplete remains False after a successful retry.'

findings.finding: The leaf unconditionally drops the relevance-exception carve-out ('unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant') that the parent epic's AC#4 explicitly preserves, directly contradicting the parent's stated acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Leaf Remediation §6 ('No relevance exception') and leaf AC 'There is no relevance exception — incomplete hierarchy is unconditionally INDETERMINATE'; vs. parent epic 6eca AC#4: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant.'
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent epic 6eca AC#4 to: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely.' (drop the 'unless' clause), then re-review the leaf. This makes the parent authoritative for the unconditional posture the leaf correctly argues for, rather than having the leaf silently override the parent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `logger.warning(...)` call (or equivalent structured log) in `_assemble_context_uncached` whenever retries are exhausted and `hierarchy_incomplete` is set, including the `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` payload, so operators have a proactive signal rather than relying solely on post-hoc coverage-dict inspection.
evidence: Remediation item 1 specifies only: 'set pctx.hierarchy_incomplete = True and pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail = ["enumeration"]' on exhaustion — no log/metric call is mentioned.
The AC bullets for retry behavior ('retries once… before setting hierarchy_incomplete=True') include no observability requirement.
'_assemble_context_uncached' is the sole assembly point; if it silently sets flags with no log, the only forensic artifact is the coverage dict stored with the verdict.
findings.finding: When `list_tickets` or `show_ticket` retries are exhausted and `hierarchy_incomplete` is set to True, no log, metric, or trace is emitted. Operators observing an INDETERMINATE verdict would have no proactive signal explaining why — they would need to inspect stored coverage dicts after the fact.
findings.location: Remediation item 1 (`orchestrator.py:163-211`); Acceptance Criteria bullet 2 and 3
scenarios: A ticket's hierarchy repeatedly fails to load (e.g., ticket store temporarily unavailable); the verdict is INDETERMINATE for hours; on-call sees no log line or metric spike to diagnose the root cause — they must correlate INDETERMINATE verdicts with coverage-dict inspection.
A per-child fetch fails silently for a specific child id; the child id appears in hierarchy_incomplete_detail but only if someone thinks to look — no alert fires.
findings.suggested_fix: After setting `pctx.hierarchy_incomplete = True` (both the enumeration and per-child paths), emit: `logger.warning("plan_review: hierarchy context incomplete after retries", extra={"ticket_id": canonical, "detail": pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail})`. This requires no new dependency and follows the structured-logging pattern likely already present in orchestrator.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Persist the fail-closed rationale (Remediation item 6's reasoning, including the `has_children`/container-criteria-suppression mechanism and the parent epic AC #4 citation) in a durable design artifact — e.g., an ADR file, an AGENTS.md section, or an inline block comment in `finalize_verdict` — not only in the ticket body.
evidence: Remediation item 6 states: 'This leaf intentionally implements the fail-closed half only… Always-INDETERMINATE is therefore the correct, narrower implementation of AC #4 for this context-assembly failure mode, not a deviation from it.' — this reasoning exists only in the ticket body.
AC bullet 9 requires only that 'the ticket body documents why', not that the rationale be persisted in a design artifact (ADR/AGENTS.md/design doc).

The plan crosses six files (orchestrator.py, det_floor.py, workflow_ops.py, gate_dispatch.py, drift_floor.py, xcheck.py) and changes a cross-cutting verdict-derivation invariant — a change of this scope typically warrants a durable design record.
findings.finding: The fail-closed architectural decision — that incomplete hierarchy context always produces INDETERMINATE with no relevance-exception carve-out, and the justification for why the carve-out's trigger condition is unstatable — is documented only inline in the ticket body (Remediation item 6). No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry is specified, leaving future maintainers without a durable rationale when the ticket is closed.
findings.location: Remediation item 6; Acceptance Criteria bullet 9 ('No relevance-exception carve-out is implemented; the ticket body documents why…')
scenarios: Six months post-merge, a developer asks 'why does an incomplete hierarchy produce INDETERMINATE rather than a degraded PASS?' — the ticket is closed, the inline rationale is inaccessible, and the code comments (if any) don't explain the parent-epic AC #4 connection.
A future ticket proposes adding a relevance-exception carve-out; without a durable ADR explaining why the carve-out's trigger condition is unstatable, the mistake is repeated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `# ARCH: ...` block comment above the new `elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete")` branch in `finalize_verdict` summarizing: (1) why incomplete context → INDETERMINATE (not PASS), (2) why no relevance carve-out is implemented (container checks are suppressed when children=[], making the carve-out trigger unstatable), and (3) the parent epic AC #4 reference. This is low-overhead and survives ticket closure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Evidence assertion about `finalize_verdict`: replace 'does not read any attribute off its `ctx` argument today' with the accurate statement that `finalize_verdict` already reads `ctx.repo_root` (line 555 for guide URL generation), and that `coverage` is the right channel for NEW behavioral flags — not because `ctx` is unused, but because the recovery/coach paths reconstruct a minimal PlanContext without live hierarchy data.
evidence: Plan states: 'it does **not** read any attribute off its `ctx: PlanContext` argument today — confirming `coverage` (not the `ctx` object) is the correct, established plumbing point'
orchestrator.py:553-555: `from rebar import config as _config` / `_base = _config.plan_review_docs_url(ctx.repo_root)` — `ctx.repo_root` is unconditionally read

The claim is used to justify the coverage-threading approach across all five call sites; a developer following the plan might incorrectly assume `ctx` is wholly unused and could break the `ctx.repo_root` usage or skip passing a ctx with a valid `repo_root`
findings.finding: `finalize_verdict` is asserted to 'not read any attribute off its `ctx: PlanContext` argument today' (Evidence section, 4th bullet), but the implementation at orchestrator.py:555 reads `ctx.repo_root` — the assertion is factually false. The plan uses this claim to justify that `coverage` (not `ctx`) is the correct plumbing point for new flags, and Remediation item 3 / AC line 7 rest on it. While the conclusion about using `coverage` is still correct, the false characterization could mislead implementers about `finalize_verdict`'s actual `ctx` consumption contract.
findings.location: Evidence section, 4th bullet ('finalize_verdict ... does not read any attribute off its ctx: PlanContext argument today'); Remediation item 3; AC item 7
scenarios: An implementer reads the plan, believes `ctx` is a no-op argument to `finalize_verdict`, and constructs a PlanContext with a bogus/None repo_root for the new `hierarchy_incomplete` branch — causing `plan_review_docs_url(ctx.repo_root)` to silently fall back or error for guide URL generation in all INDETERMINATE verdicts emitted via the new branch.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Evidence bullet, change: 'it does not read any attribute off its ctx: PlanContext argument today' → 'it reads only ctx.repo_root (for guide URL generation, line 555) and no hierarchy-related attributes — confirming that coverage is the correct channel for hierarchy_incomplete, since recovery paths reconstruct a minimal PlanContext without live hierarchy data'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the exact insertion position in `finalize_verdict`'s elif chain: the `hierarchy_incomplete` branch must be placed immediately after the `verify_failed` branch and BEFORE the `elif indeterminate and not surfaced` branch, so it fires for any non-blocking incomplete-hierarchy verdict regardless of the indeterminate/surfaced mix.
evidence: Remediation item 3: 'add elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE" alongside the existing llm_unavailable/verify_failed branches (same elif chain, checked before falling through to PASS)'

orchestrator.py:525-541: chain is if blocking / elif llm_unavailable / elif verify_failed / elif indeterminate and not surfaced / else PASS — the new branch must be between verify_failed and indeterminate-and-not-surfaced to be correct
ABSENCE: plan does not specify the exact insertion position in the chain, only 'alongside llm_unavailable/verify_failed' and 'before PASS'
findings.finding: The plan's instruction to add `elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"` in `finalize_verdict`'s elif chain ('alongside the existing llm_unavailable/verify_failed branches, checked before falling through to PASS') is position-ambiguous relative to the existing `elif indeterminate and not surfaced` branch (line 538). If the implementer places the new branch AFTER that check — which is natural, since both resolve to INDETERMINATE — a ticket with `hierarchy_incomplete=True`, no blocking, `verify_failed=False`, `llm_unavailable=False`, `indeterminate=[]`, and non-empty `surfaced` advisories would reach `else: verdict = "PASS"` because neither the `indeterminate and not surfaced` branch (false: indeterminate is empty) nor the hierarchy_incomplete branch (never reached after else) fires first.
findings.location: Remediation item 3 / orchestrator.py:525-541 (finalize_verdict elif chain)
scenarios: An incomplete-hierarchy ticket that successfully ran the LLM and produced only advisory (surfaced) findings — hierarchy_incomplete=True, blocking=[], indeterminate=[], surfaced=[...] — would fall through the chain to else: PASS if the branch is placed after the indeterminate check instead of before it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Remediation item 3: 'Insert as the third elif, immediately after the verify_failed branch (line ~529) and before the `elif indeterminate and not surfaced` branch (line ~538). Order becomes: if blocking / elif llm_unavailable / elif verify_failed / elif hierarchy_incomplete / elif indeterminate and not surfaced / else PASS.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the bounded retry in Remediation item 1 — state what class of transient failures it targets (e.g., I/O blips on a local or networked ticket store) and why a single retry with a short fixed delay is proportionate given the broad-exception predicate and no transient/permanent error split in `_reads.py`.
evidence: Evidence: '_reads.show_ticket/list_tickets raise a single broad RebarError on any failure (no typed transient/permanent split)'

Remediation item 1: 'wrap the list_tickets call (line 174) in a bounded retry: 2 attempts total (1 retry), catching the same broad Exception it catches today, with a fixed 50ms delay' — no rationale stated for why retry helps given undifferentiated errors
ABSENCE: no statement explaining what class of transient failures the retry targets or why 50ms is appropriate for a local ticket-store read
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for why a bounded retry (2 attempts, 50ms fixed delay) is the right strategy for `list_tickets`/`show_ticket` failures, given that `_reads.py` raises a single broad `RebarError` with no transient/permanent distinction. The Evidence section explains only why existing retriers don't transfer (negative/exclusionary reasoning), not why retrying a permanently-failing call improves outcomes over a fail-fast-and-flag approach. Without a stated rationale, an implementer cannot judge when to deviate (e.g., if the store becomes remote and 50ms is too tight, or if errors are never transient in practice).
findings.location: Remediation item 1 / Evidence section (retry precedent paragraph)
scenarios: The ticket store is on a network filesystem with occasional ETIMEDOUT errors — retry helps and 50ms is appropriate.
The ticket store has a permanent corruption — retry wastes 50ms on every call but doesn't break correctness (benign).
Future maintainer encounters a different failure mode and cannot tell from the plan text whether the retry is essential to the design or incidental.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update parent epic AC #4 to remove the relevance-exception clause (and re-review the parent before re-reviewing this leaf), OR add a relevance-exception implementation path to this leaf — the leaf may not unilaterally eliminate a carve-out the parent's AC text explicitly provides.
evidence: Parent AC #4 verbatim: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely, unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant.' — the 'unless' clause is an explicit exception the parent grants.

Leaf Remediation item 6: 'This leaf intentionally implements the fail-closed half only, with no relevance-exception carve-out' and 'Always-INDETERMINATE is therefore the correct, narrower implementation of AC #4 for this context-assembly failure mode, not a deviation from it.'

Leaf AC (final bullet): 'No relevance-exception carve-out is implemented; the ticket body documents why (Remediation item 6), citing parent epic 6eca-183e-1cd2-4fb9 AC #4 verbatim and the has_children/container-criteria-suppression mechanism that makes the carve-out\'s trigger condition unstatable.'

Per the CONFLICT RULE, the parent wins on any conflict. The leaf's argument that the carve-out is 'vacuous by construction' is an engineering conclusion that, if correct, should be made by updating the parent's AC — not by diverging the leaf implementation from the parent's explicit text.
findings.finding: The leaf explicitly removes the relevance-exception carve-out that parent epic AC #4 includes ('unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant'), substituting an always-INDETERMINATE rule. The parent's AC text affirmatively reserves this exception; the leaf cannot unilaterally eliminate it — even with a reasoned justification in Remediation item 6.
findings.location: Remediation item 6 / Acceptance Criteria line 9 ('No relevance-exception carve-out is implemented')
scenarios: A reviewer or future implementer reads parent AC #4 and expects a relevance exception to be implementable; the leaf has permanently foreclosed that path without a parent update.

If the parent's reasoning is revisited (e.g., a new context-assembly path is added where has_children is not the only gating mechanism), the undocumented deviation from AC #4's 'unless' clause will be invisible in the parent, creating silent contract drift.
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent epic `6eca-183e-1cd2-4fb9` AC #4 to read: 'Plan review cannot produce an ordinary clean pass when the ticket hierarchy was loaded incompletely.' (removing 'unless the omitted context is demonstrably irrelevant'), with a note referencing this leaf's Remediation item 6 argument as the rationale for dropping the carve-out. Then re-review the parent. Only after the parent's AC matches the leaf's implementation is the leaf in compliance with G7.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'There are exactly two `finalize_verdict` call sites in this package: call site 1 at line 209, inside `plan_review_precheck`... call site 2 at line 557, inside `plan_review_coach`' — but a grep reveals three additional call sites in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` at lines 336 (`_recover_plan_review_coach_failure`), 396 (`_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`), and 431 (`_degraded_plan_review_verdict`). All three build bare `PlanContext` objects and assemble `coverage` dicts that read `precheck.get('det_coverage')` but would not carry `hierarchy_incomplete` under the remediation as written. This means the new `hierarchy_incomplete → INDETERMINATE` guard would be silently bypassed on the coach-failure, verify-failure, and LLM-outage recovery paths.
findings.location: Evidence section / Plumbing precedent — 'There are exactly two `finalize_verdict` call sites in this package'
findings.suggested_fix: In each of the three `gate_dispatch.py` functions, add `'hierarchy_incomplete': precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete') or False, 'hierarchy_incomplete_detail': precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete_detail') or []` to the `coverage` dict assembled before calling `finalize_verdict`. Update AC item 3 to name all five call sites.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'There are exactly two `finalize_verdict` call sites in this package' (naming only `workflow_ops.py:209` and `workflow_ops.py:557`), but `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` contains three additional call sites (lines 336 in `_recover_plan_review_coach_failure`, 396 in `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`, and 431 in `_degraded_plan_review_verdict`). Each builds a `coverage` dict from raw `precheck` step outputs — not via the `ctx.inputs` threading the plan specifies — so none would receive the new `hierarchy_incomplete` signal. These are unenumerated consumers of `finalize_verdict` that require the same coverage-threading update; omitting them means the new `INDETERMINATE` guard is bypassed whenever the workflow degrades through a coach-failure or verify-failure recovery.
findings.location: Evidence section ('There are exactly two `finalize_verdict` call sites in this package') and Remediation step 3 / AC item 3 (threading spec covers only `workflow_ops.py:209` and `workflow_ops.py:557`)
findings.suggested_fix: In each of the three recovery functions in `gate_dispatch.py`, after extracting `precheck` outputs, add: `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete'] = precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete') or False` and `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete_detail'] = precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete_detail') or []` — mirroring the `det_coverage` threading pattern already present in each function (`precheck.get('det_coverage') or {}`).

findings.finding: The plan specifies threading `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` through `finalize_verdict` at exactly two call sites (`workflow_ops.py:209` and `workflow_ops.py:557`), but `gate_dispatch.py` contains three additional `finalize_verdict` call sites (lines 336, 396, 431) in recovery paths (`_recover_plan_review_coach_failure`, `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`, `_degraded_plan_review_verdict`) that each reconstruct a `coverage` dict from `precheck` step outputs using the same `precheck.get('det_coverage')` pattern. Without extending the threading spec to these paths, a hierarchy-incomplete run that enters any of the three recovery/degrade branches — coach failure mid-tail, verify exhaustion, or full LLM-tier outage — will emit a verdict that lacks `coverage['hierarchy_incomplete']`, allowing `finalize_verdict` to return PASS (or a non-INDETERMINATE) even when the hierarchy context was incomplete.
findings.location: Remediation step 3 / AC line: `plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:503-564)` / `gate_dispatch.py:298-433`
findings.suggested_fix: In all three `gate_dispatch.py` recovery functions, add `'hierarchy_incomplete': precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete') or False, 'hierarchy_incomplete_detail': precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete_detail') or []` to the `coverage` dict built before calling `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`, mirroring the plan's spec for `plan_review_coach`. Add a corresponding AC bullet and a test covering a coach-failure recovery on a hierarchy-incomplete ticket.

findings.finding: The plan threads `hierarchy_incomplete` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` through `plan_review_precheck`'s step-output dict (Remediation step 3, AC bullet 3) but never specifies updating `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json`, which has `"additionalProperties": false` and a closed `required` list. The workflow reference linter (`lint_refs.py:448-464`) builds its output contract from that schema's `properties` — fields absent from the schema are flagged UNKNOWN, and any schema-validating consumer rejects the output dict.
findings.location: Remediation step 3 / AC bullet 3 / src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` under `"properties"`: `"hierarchy_incomplete": {"type": "boolean", "description": "True when child enumeration or per-child detail loading failed after retries."}` and `"hierarchy_incomplete_detail": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Which hierarchy reads failed: child ids or 'enumeration' for a total list failure."}`. Also add both to `required` (or change to `"additionalProperties": true` to match the pattern used by `plan_review_coach_input.schema.json`).

findings.finding: The AC's threading criterion states that `plan_review_coach` consumes the new fields via `ctx.inputs.get(...)` 'at both `finalize_verdict` call sites (`workflow_ops.py:209` and `workflow_ops.py:557`)', but the Evidence and Remediation step 3 establish that call site 1 (line 209) is inside `plan_review_precheck` (not `plan_review_coach`) and the real `PlanContext` is in scope there — so it reads `pctx.hierarchy_incomplete` directly, not via `ctx.inputs`. The AC as written sends the implementer in two directions: follow the AC literally (have `plan_review_coach` somehow handle line 209) or follow the Remediation (two different mechanisms for two different functions).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 3 ('plan_review_precheck threads...and plan_review_coach...consumes them...at both `finalize_verdict` call sites (`workflow_ops.py:209` and `workflow_ops.py:557`)')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC item 3 parenthetical with: 'At call site 1 (workflow_ops.py:209, inside plan_review_precheck), read pctx.hierarchy_incomplete directly since the real PlanContext is in scope. At call site 2 (workflow_ops.py:557, inside plan_review_coach), read ctx.inputs.get("hierarchy_incomplete") and ctx.inputs.get("hierarchy_incomplete_detail") — exactly how ctx.inputs.get("det_coverage") is read at line 547 — and fold both into the coverage dict passed to finalize_verdict.'

findings.finding: The test cases listed in the AC verify that `hierarchy_incomplete` is set (bool) and that `finalize_verdict`/`_recompute_verdict_after_drop` return INDETERMINATE, but no test case asserts on the CONTENT of `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` — the detail list that is supposed to record specific failed child IDs or the literal string `"enumeration"` for a total list failure. Since this field is explicitly specified as a required AC ("The INDETERMINATE verdict's coverage detail identifies which specific hierarchy reads failed or were omitted") and serves as the key operator-facing diagnostic, shipping with an always-empty or incorrectly-populated detail field would satisfy all listed tests while silently breaking the diagnostic guarantee.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet ("Tests cover: ...") and second-to-last bullet ("The INDETERMINATE verdict's coverage detail identifies which specific hierarchy reads failed or were omitted")
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the existing 'total child-list failure' test to assert `pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ["enumeration"]` (or equivalently that `coverage["hierarchy_incomplete_detail"] == ["enumeration"]` at the finalize_verdict call site). Extend the 'one-child detail failure among several' test to assert the failed child's ID appears in `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` and successful children's IDs do not.

findings.finding: AC3 contradicts Remediation Step 3 on which step reads which call site and how: AC3 states that 'plan_review_decide consumes them via ctx.inputs.get(...) at both finalize_verdict call sites (~209 and ~557),' but Remediation Step 3 is explicit that call site 1 (~209) is inside plan_review_precheck (not plan_review_decide) and reads from `pctx.hierarchy_incomplete` directly (not via `ctx.inputs.get()`). An implementer reading only the AC would wire call site 1 incorrectly — reading from `ctx.inputs` inside plan_review_decide instead of reading `pctx` directly inside plan_review_precheck.
findings.location: AC3 vs. Remediation Step 3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3 with two sub-bullets: (a) 'plan_review_precheck adds hierarchy_incomplete/hierarchy_incomplete_detail to its step-output dict; at its own finalize_verdict call site (~line 209) it reads pctx.hierarchy_incomplete directly when building the coverage dict.' (b) 'plan_review_decide reads hierarchy_incomplete/hierarchy_incomplete_detail via ctx.inputs.get(...) — exactly as it reads ctx.inputs.get("det_coverage") — and folds them into the coverage dict at its finalize_verdict call site (~line 557).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address the `_exempt_verdict` path (`workflow_ops.py:150-157`) — either document it as an intentional carve-out (with the same rationale rigor as Remediation item 6) or ensure `hierarchy_incomplete=True` on an exempt ticket still produces INDETERMINATE rather than the hardcoded PASS.
evidence: Remediation item 6: 'No relevance-exception carve-out is implemented'
workflow_ops.py:150-157: returns orchestrator._exempt_verdict(...) which hardcodes 'verdict': 'PASS' with coverage={'exempt': reason}, never consulting hierarchy_incomplete
orchestrator.py:721-735: _exempt_verdict builds the verdict dict directly with 'verdict': 'PASS', no coverage.get() checks
Plan lists exactly 5 finalize_verdict call sites; _exempt_verdict is a separate code path producing verdicts without going through finalize_verdict at all
findings.finding: The `_exempt_verdict` exit path (session_log, code_review, identity types) at `workflow_ops.py:150-157` hardcodes `"verdict": "PASS"` and bypasses `finalize_verdict` entirely — so `hierarchy_incomplete=True` on these ticket types is silently ignored even though the plan declares "No relevance-exception carve-out" and "Always-INDETERMINATE is therefore the correct implementation". The plan enumerates exactly five `finalize_verdict` call sites but says nothing about this sixth exit, leaving an uncovered escape path where a transient store failure during context assembly of an exempt ticket would still produce a clean PASS.
findings.location: Remediation items 2–3 / workflow_ops.py:149-157 (_exempt_verdict path)
scenarios: A session_log or code_review ticket whose list_tickets call fails transiently — hierarchy_incomplete is set on pctx, but the type-gate fires first and _exempt_verdict produces a clean PASS, silently hiding the storage failure.
Any exempt-type ticket reviewed during a transient store degradation reaches a signed PASS, undermining the plan's stated always-INDETERMINATE guarantee for hierarchy_incomplete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit position of the new `hierarchy_incomplete` elif branch relative to the existing `llm_unavailable` and `verify_failed` branches in both `finalize_verdict` and `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` (e.g., 'insert after `verify_failed`, before the PASS fallthrough').
evidence: 'add `elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"` alongside the existing `llm_unavailable`/`verify_failed` branches (same `elif` chain, checked before falling through to PASS)' — 'alongside' and 'before falling through to PASS' specifies position relative to PASS but not relative to the other two branches

Same gap in Remediation item 4 for drift_floor: 'add the identical `elif cov.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict["verdict"] = "INDETERMINATE"` branch' with no ordering anchor against existing branches
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding `elif coverage.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` in `finalize_verdict` 'alongside' the existing `llm_unavailable`/`verify_failed` branches, and the identical branch in `_recompute_verdict_after_drop`, but never states the relative ordering of this new branch among the existing elif branches. When two flags are simultaneously True (e.g., both `hierarchy_incomplete` and `llm_unavailable`), whichever branch comes first wins — this is an unspecified precedence decision.
findings.location: Remediation items 3 and 4; AC lines 'finalize_verdict…gains an elif' and 'drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop…gains an elif'
scenarios: An executor places the new branch before `llm_unavailable`, making INDETERMINATE override llm_unavailable — or after, making llm_unavailable override INDETERMINATE. Both are plausible readings; the outcome differs in simultaneous-flag edge cases.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation items 3 and 4, replace 'alongside the existing … branches' with a positional anchor, e.g.: 'Insert after the `elif coverage.get("verify_failed")` branch and before the PASS fallthrough, so that llm_unavailable and verify_failed take precedence when multiple flags are set.' Mirror this wording in Remediation item 4 for drift_floor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an explicit rollback/back-out path for the PASS→INDETERMINATE behavioral change — either a feature flag (e.g., `HIERARCHY_INCOMPLETE_FAILS_CLOSED=true`) that preserves the prior silent-pass behavior when disabled, or a documented revert-and-redeploy procedure with the exact steps and estimated time-to-revert.
evidence: Remediation item 3: 'add elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"' — this is a hard behavioral switch with no toggle.

Remediation item 1 specifies only a single 1-retry mitigation (2 attempts, 50ms) against transient failures; if the ticket store is degraded for minutes, every plan review in that window becomes INDETERMINATE.

ABSENCE: No section of the plan mentions a feature flag, a canary rollout, or a revert procedure. The plan's Evidence section explicitly rules out a broader retry helper (dispatch_one.py's _call_with_retry) as inapplicable, making the new retry code bespoke and untested in production.
findings.finding: The plan changes an existing behavioral guarantee — hierarchy-load failures previously produced silent PASS verdicts; after this change they produce INDETERMINATE. This affects all five finalize_verdict call sites and every active plan-review workflow. While the change is extensively justified, the plan specifies no rollback path, feature flag, or back-out procedure should the change cause operational problems (e.g., transient ticket-store instability causing widespread INDETERMINATE verdicts despite the retry).
findings.location: Remediation item 3 / Acceptance Criteria (AC #7) / Finding section
scenarios: Ticket store experiences a sustained degradation; every plan review produces INDETERMINATE for the outage duration; no fast rollback path exists without a code revert and redeploy.
The bespoke 50ms/2-attempt retry is insufficient for the actual transient failure mode; operational teams have no flag to revert to the prior silent-pass behavior while a fix is prepared.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Rollback section: 'If widespread INDETERMINATE verdicts are observed after deploy, revert commits X–Y and redeploy; estimated time: <N> minutes. Alternatively, set env var HIERARCHY_INCOMPLETE_FAILS_CLOSED=false to restore prior behavior without a redeploy — finalize_verdict reads this flag before applying the INDETERMINATE branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback schema-state assertion: specify that either (a) the two new fields are added to `properties` before any enforcement of `additionalProperties: false` is re-tightened, or (b) the rollback procedure includes a compensating-forward step (e.g., strip the new fields from persisted records, or temporarily relax `additionalProperties` to `true` during the rollback window) — and explicitly note `DATA LOSS RISK` if neither path is taken.
evidence: "Leave `additionalProperties: false` as-is (both new keys are now declared)" — plan Remediation item 5
Plan is silent on rollback schema state for already-persisted records containing the new fields
AC #8: schema declares new fields but does not address what happens when those records are re-read after rollback
findings.finding: The plan preserves `additionalProperties: false` in `plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` and adds the two new fields only to `properties` (not `required`). On a code rollback, the schema file reverts to its pre-change state (without `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` in `properties`), but any step-output records already persisted with those fields will now fail strict `additionalProperties: false` validation — potentially breaking all recovery/degraded-path reads of those records. The plan does not assert the schema STATE after rollback, nor provide a compensating-forward strategy for in-flight records.
findings.location: Remediation item 5 / `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json`
scenarios: A deployment writes several `plan_review_precheck` step-output records that include `hierarchy_incomplete: true`. An incident triggers a rollback. The schema file reverts, dropping `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` from `properties` while `additionalProperties: false` is still enforced. Any workflow recovery path (`_recover_plan_review_coach_failure`, `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`) that re-reads those precheck records now receives a schema-validation failure, surfacing as an opaque error rather than a graceful INDETERMINATE verdict.
DATA LOSS RISK: if the schema validator gates record consumption, in-flight verdict computations for those workflows are lost, not merely degraded.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation item 5 and the relevant AC, add: 'Rollback note: if the deployment is reverted, any step-output records already written with `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` will fail `additionalProperties: false` validation under the old schema. Compensating-forward strategy: keep the new field declarations in `properties` even on rollback (schema-only change is safe to leave in place), OR set `additionalProperties: true` as a temporary bridge. Do not revert the schema file before draining in-flight workflow executions that may have written precheck outputs with the new fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend Remediation item 2 to cover the `bug`-tier early-return path in `plan_review_precheck` (lines 166–178): thread `hierarchy_incomplete` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` from `pctx` into the returned dict (and into the `base` dict written before the tier branches), so all five downstream `finalize_verdict` recovery consumers receive the flag regardless of ticket type.
evidence: Bug-tier path (lines 166-178) returns `{**base, 'det_blocking': [], 'det_advisory': [...], 'det_coverage': {...}, 'run_llm': True, 'verdict': None}` — no `hierarchy_incomplete` or `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` key.

Remediation item 2 says 'plan_review_precheck, call site line 209: real pctx in scope -> coverage["hierarchy_incomplete"] = pctx.hierarchy_incomplete' — but line 209 is only reached via the non-bug, non-exempt, non-p8-too-big code path; the bug-tier return at line 171 exits before reaching it.

Gate_dispatch recovery paths use `precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` (Remediation item 2, `_recover_plan_review_coach_failure` and `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`) — a missing key silently resolves to `False`, suppressing any incomplete-hierarchy signal for bug tickets.
ABSENCE: Remediation items 2, 3, 4, and AC make no mention of the bug-tier or exempt-tier early-return paths that bypass `finalize_verdict` entirely.
findings.finding: The `bug` tier early-return path in `plan_review_precheck` (workflow_ops.py lines 166–178) returns a precheck dict WITHOUT threading `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail`, leaving a structural gap: if a bug ticket's context assembly fails, recovery paths in gate_dispatch (`_recover_plan_review_coach_failure`, `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`) will silently use the `.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` default and never surface the incompleteness as INDETERMINATE.
findings.location: workflow_ops.py lines 166–178 (bug-tier early return); Remediation item 2 / AC: `plan_review_precheck (workflow_ops.py:135) reads pctx.hierarchy_incomplete…`
scenarios: A bug ticket's `list_tickets` call fails (e.g. ticket store unavailable) during context assembly. `_assemble_context_uncached` sets `hierarchy_incomplete=True`. `plan_review_precheck` takes the bug-tier branch, returns a dict without `hierarchy_incomplete`. The LLM review runs. Later, if a recovery path is triggered, `precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` → `False`, so the INDETERMINATE verdict is never emitted despite the incomplete hierarchy.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` injection into the `base` dict construction at lines 141–147 (before any tier branch), rather than only at the `finalize_verdict` call site at line 209. Since `base` is merged into every return path, all tier branches — bug, exempt, p8-too-big, and normal — will automatically carry the flags without per-branch changes.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
